bibliographic_data.title: Pointwise density estimation on metric spaces and applications in seismology
bibliographic_data.abstract: We are studying the problem of estimating density in a wide range of metric spaces, including the Euclidean space, the sphere, the ball, and various Riemannian manifolds. Our framework involves a metric space with a doubling measure and a self-adjoint operator, whose heat kernel exhibits Gaussian behavior. We begin by reviewing the construction of kernel density estimators and the related background information. As a novel result, we present a pointwise kernel density estimation for probability density functions that belong to general H¨older spaces. The study is accompanied by an application in Seismology. Precisely, we analyze a globally-indexed dataset of earthquake occurrence and compare the out-of-sample performance of several approximated kernel density estimators indexed on the sphere.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a pointwise kernel density estimation for probability density functions that belong to general H¨older spaces and applies this method in seismology to analyze earthquake occurrence data.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use a metric space with a doubling measure and a self-adjoint operator, whose heat kernel exhibits Gaussian behavior. They construct kernel density estimators and apply them to the sphere for out-of-sample performance comparison.
outcomes: Developed pointwise kernel density estimation for H¨older spaces
Applied method in seismology to analyze earthquake occurrence data
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we explore the problem of estimating density in a wide range of metric spaces, including Euclidean space, the sphere, and various Riemannian manifolds. The framework involves a metric space with a doubling measure and a self-adjoint operator whose heat kernel exhibits Gaussian behavior. We review the construction of kernel density estimators and present novel results on pointwise estimation for probability density functions in H¨older spaces. Additionally, we apply our method to seismology by analyzing earthquake occurrence data on the sphere, comparing out-of-sample performance of different approximated kernel density estimators.
